original_hypothesis.title: Unrestricted ContributionNft minting enables on-chain spoofing of rejected/active proposals into the contribution graph
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: ContributionNft.mint() only checks that the caller is the DAO-reported proposalProposer(proposalId), but does not require the proposal to be Succeeded/Accepted. A proposer can mint a Contribution NFT for an active or defeated proposal, polluting the on-chain contribution tree (parents/children, cores, model/dataset flags) and any downstream logic that treats ContributionNft existence as proof of acceptance.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The protocol’s contribution history and reputation system rely on ContributionNft as an authoritative record. If proposers can mint regardless of governance outcome, attackers can create fake lineage, spam cores, and potentially influence any reward/indexing logic that keys off the contribution graph or tokenURI metadata.

proof_of_signal.pattern: mint(): require(msg.sender == personaDAO.proposalProposer(proposalId)) but no require(isAccepted(proposalId)) / no governor.state(proposalId) check

preconditions: Attacker is the proposer address for proposalId in the relevant AgentDAO
ContributionNft contract is configured as the protocol’s canonical contribution registry for that AgentNft

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Proposer can mint contribution NFTs immediately after proposing (before votes conclude) or after defeat, creating accepted-looking artifacts
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after proposalId exists and proposalProposer(proposalId) is set

operational_questions: Do any on-chain/off-chain reward, ranking, or UI components treat ContributionNft existence as acceptance?
Can minted-but-unaccepted ContributionNfts be used as parents for subsequent contributions to create fake trees?
Are there cleanup/blacklist mechanisms for invalid ContributionNfts?
validation_requirements: Attempt mint() for proposal states: Pending/Active/Defeated/Canceled and confirm it succeeds
Trace all consumers of ContributionNft (ServiceNft, rewards, indexers) to see whether acceptance is revalidated

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Create proposals that will not pass (or remain Active)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call ContributionNft.mint() for those proposalIds to create NFTs and graph edges (parent/children)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Use the minted NFTs to mislead indexers/UIs and potentially influence any reward pipelines tied to contribution artifacts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Scale via repeated proposals to spam a given virtualId/core with fake contributions
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Low; attacker only needs proposer rights

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: High integrity loss of contribution history; increased moderation burden; potential reward leakage if downstream systems rely on minted contributions
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Medium; requires off-chain filtering or an on-chain blacklist/cleanup migration
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: Contribution attribution, service mirroring assumptions, any analytics/rewards keyed off ContributionNft

execution_requirements_analysis.governance_tokens_needed: None beyond whatever is needed to propose (could be bypassed if proposer is ContributionNft admin per AgentDAO logic)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: ContributionNft NewContribution events for proposals whose governor state != Succeeded at mint time
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: High volume of contribution mints with no corresponding passed proposals
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Single proposer minting many NFTs across cores/virtualIds

validation_result.mechanism: Missing proposal state validation in ContributionNft.mint()
validation_result.impact_summary: Proposers can mint ContributionNft for proposals in any state (Active, Defeated, Canceled), polluting the on-chain contribution graph (parent/child trees, cores, model/dataset flags) and misleading downstream consumers like ServiceNft, reward systems, and indexers.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The ContributionNft.mint() function (lines 55-92) performs only two checks: (1) msg.sender == personaDAO.proposalProposer(proposalId), and (2) parentId != proposalId. It does NOT verify that the proposal has succeeded through governance voting. The contract itself contains an isAccepted() view function (lines 48-52) that checks personaDAO.state(tokenId) == IGovernor.ProposalState.Succeeded, but this function is never called within mint(). This means any proposer can mint a ContributionNft immediately after creating a proposal, before voting even starts, or after a proposal has been defeated. The minted NFT populates the contribution tree (_parents, _children mappings), core assignments, and model/dataset flags, all of which are read by downstream contracts like AgentDAO._updateMaturity and ServiceNft.mint.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. Lines 55-92 of ContributionNft.sol show the mint function with only a proposalProposer check and no proposal state check. Lines 48-52 show the isAccepted() function exists but is unused during minting. This is a clear missing validation defect.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function only checks msg.sender == proposalProposer(proposalId) but never checks proposal state. The isAccepted() function exists at lines 48-52 but is never invoked in the mint path. A proposer can mint for Active, Defeated, or Canceled proposals.

This function exists as a view-only check but is never called by mint(). Its existence actually reinforces that the developers considered acceptance checking important but failed to enforce it in the write path.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker creates a proposal via AgentDAO.propose() for a given virtualId
2. Attacker immediately calls ContributionNft.mint() with the proposalId (proposal is still Active/Pending)
4. mint() checks msg.sender == personaDAO.proposalProposer(proposalId) -> passes since attacker is the proposer (line 66-69)
6. No proposal state check occurs - isAccepted() is never called
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is at ContributionNft.sol:55-92. After the proposalProposer check at line 66-69, execution proceeds directly to _mint at line 72 without any state check. The isAccepted() function at line 48-52 is defined but never invoked. The attacker can mint for a proposal in any state (Pending, Active, Defeated, Canceled, Expired). This pollutes _children[parentId] (line 77), _cores (line 78), modelContributions (line 81), and modelDatasets (line 82). Downstream, AgentDAO._updateMaturity (AgentDAO.sol line ~155) reads IERC721(contributionNft).ownerOf(proposalId) and IContributionNft(contributionNft).isModel(proposalId), both of which will return valid data for a fraudulently minted NFT.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script v1/deployContributions.ts shows ContributionNft deployed as a proxy with initialize([VIRTUAL_NFT]) and setAdmin(ADMIN). No additional state checks are configured. The contract is the canonical contribution registry set via nft.setContributionService() in v1/deployService.ts.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack is straightforward and highly realistic: (1) Attacker proposes a contribution via AgentDAO.propose(). They need enough voting power to meet proposalThreshold OR be the ContributionNft admin (who bypasses threshold per AgentDAO.propose line ~100-103). (2) Without waiting for voting to conclude, attacker calls ContributionNft.mint() with the proposalId. The only check is proposalProposer which passes. (3) The NFT is minted, and the contribution graph is polluted with parent/child relationships, core assignments, and model/dataset flags for an unaccepted proposal. (4) Even if the proposal is later Defeated, the NFT persists on-chain. (5) Any downstream system treating ContributionNft existence as proof of acceptance is misled. AgentDAO._updateMaturity checks ownerOf(proposalId) != address(0) to determine if a contribution exists (line ~155-158), and this check passes for fraudulently minted NFTs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: HIGH integrity impact. The contribution graph is a core protocol data structure. Fraudulent NFTs can: (1) Create fake parent-child lineage via _children[parentId].push(proposalId), (2) Register fake model contributions via modelContributions[proposalId] = true, (3) Associate arbitrary datasets via modelDatasets[proposalId] = datasetId, (4) Pollute core assignments via _cores[proposalId] = coreId, (5) Influence AgentDAO._updateMaturity calculations for legitimate proposals that check ownerOf on the contribution NFT, (6) Mislead the ServiceNft system which reads ContributionNft data (getCore, isModel, getDatasetId) during minting, (7) Affect reward distribution systems (v1/deployReward.ts references CONTRIBUTION_NFT as a parameter).
recommended_mitigations: Add a proposal state check in ContributionNft.mint(): require(personaDAO.state(proposalId) == IGovernor.ProposalState.Succeeded || personaDAO.state(proposalId) == IGovernor.ProposalState.Executed, 'Proposal not accepted');
Alternatively, restrict minting to be callable only during proposal execution (via the DAO's execute flow) rather than directly by the proposer
Add a burn/invalidation mechanism for ContributionNfts minted for proposals that are later defeated or canceled
Downstream consumers (rewards, indexers) should additionally validate proposal state via isAccepted() rather than relying solely on NFT existence
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy ContributionNft proxy with a mock AgentNft. 2. Deploy AgentDAO with a test token. 3. Create a proposal via AgentDAO.propose(). 4. Before any votes are cast (proposal is Active), call ContributionNft.mint() from the proposer address. 5. Verify the NFT was minted successfully. 6. Cast Against votes to defeat the proposal. 7. Verify the NFT still exists and isAccepted() returns false. 8. Verify _children, _cores, modelContributions mappings contain the fraudulent data.
uncertainties: The exact downstream financial impact depends on reward distribution contracts (AgentReward, Minter) which reference CONTRIBUTION_NFT but their full source is not provided in scope
The proposalThreshold requirement in AgentDAO.propose() may limit who can create proposals, but the ContributionNft admin can bypass this threshold
additional_testing_needed: Verify whether AgentReward/Minter contracts treat ContributionNft existence as proof of acceptance without additional state checks
Test whether minting with arbitrary parentId values for non-existent parents corrupts the contribution tree traversal
Verify if the same proposalId can be used across different virtualIds to create conflicting contribution records
